sentences.text: I arrived in Nuremberg on November 6th, 1946.

As a matter of fact, on the way there, the plane landed in Frankfurt, and some military officers came on board to try to get me off the plane to work on other, evidentiary hearings involving the trials in Frankfurt.
So I had a little verbal tussle with them about getting on to Nuremberg, which was where I was going.
I actually had to get to a point and say, I have a signed contract to go to Nuremberg.
Nuremberg, or send me back to the United States.
I don't know how I had the nerve to do that, you know, but I did, and I got to Nuremberg.
It was a bombed-out city.
Totally cold, ice cold, snow.
And I was assigned my first home in Nuremberg, which was on [?
Beulah ?] Strasse.
It was a three-bedroom, ordinary, two-story house, which I-- which I was-- I shared for several months with one young British woman, and one young Frenchwoman on the Allied payroll.
But they were not court reporters, different categories.
And we were picked up every morning at our front door by-- which was the order.
We were-- we were really restricted and limited.
We had no freedom, because of the Nazi fanatics who were still around in catacombs in Nuremberg, and the threat of being killed, bombings and shootings, which had already occurred in Stuttgart.
So we were picked up every morning at our front door by a displaced person who was on the Allied payroll.
He usually came in a German car, and drove us directly to the iron gates of the Palace of Justice, which were guarded by United States Army personnel carriers, US troops.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's kind of how-- Who was your immediate supervisor when you first arrived?
When I first arrived, I-- my immediate-- immediate-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I reported to a woman, as I remember first, Captain Sara Kruskell, S-A-R-A K-R-U-S-K-A-L-L, a-- a WAC captain.
And my immediate court reporting supervisor was the Chief of Reporters, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Charles Foster, of somewhere in northern California.
I'm sure that we were.
I-- I do not remember specifically.
But I-- I had to become aware of the fact that we were subject to military control.

We had to follow military orders, and when they were given-- such as you will not go out on the street and walk around Nuremberg after-- you will not, after 7:00 at night, especially, when it was getting dark.
And I was aware that-- I was told that we were going to be a team of about six reporters on the Doctors' Trial.
I was told I was going to work the Doctors' Trial.
That was the most important.
That's the trial I wanted above all trials.
Well, because the-- Goering and all of the major Nazi leaders were what I called the political animals.
And the doctors were the scientists, the educated scientists, who had taken the Hippocratic oath to heal and cure.
And I had learned by that time enough of what was going on in these camps, somehow.
I'm sure that we-- there was-- that there were orientation sessions for us.
I'm sure they involved some kind of film that the US Army had us attend.
I don't remember specifically.
Well, I was to be part of the court reporting team.
And we were assigned to a large office.
The other trial had ended.
Goering's trial had ended in-- on August 31st, 1946.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Goering had committed suicide in October--
October 10 or 16?
My golly--
Yes.
It was October 16th. 10 of the major Nazi defendants had been sentenced to death by hanging, including Martin Bormann in absentia.
And on October 16th-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] October 16th, four hours before Goering was to be hanged, he swallowed a cyanide capsule, which was a terrible shock to everyone in Nuremberg.
Gary, I am-- you know, thinking, I hope I'm getting my figures right here about the major Nazi leaders who were sentenced to death by hanging.
On the trials.
And I-- and I'm thinking now that it was 12 sentenced to death by hanging, and 10 were hanged.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I actually must have started working on the trials, I would say, within three or four days of the time I arrived.
The Nazi Doctors' Trial actually started on October 25, 1946.
I arrived in Nuremberg on November 6.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do not remember whether the actual opening statements had started.
The indictments had been filed.
All of the pleas had been taken, I believe.
I-- I just do not remember.
But I know I was right in with the other reporters, and I was happy to be on the Doctors' Trial while other reporters in that office-- and we had about 24 to 26-- were assigned to other trials.
[SIGHS] The whole environment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seemed to be filled with some kind of horror.
And the courtroom at the Palace of Justice had been bombed, and there was extensive damage, I understood.
But so it was all rebuilt and corrected for the main-- certainly for the main courtroom of the Palace of Justice.
It was a huge room, and I was familiar with courtrooms in the United States, both in state and federal courts.
And it-- the paneling, it was beautifully paneled, huge room.
We had-- there were double doors entering the courtroom, and US Army guards.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to show our top security passes to get into the courtroom.
There was no heat at all in Germany, no heat.
And so I remember-- outside of in fireplaces or little plug-in heaters into the wall-- but I remember that we were our coats to the courtroom.
And we had wool clothes, layers of sweaters.
And we took our coats off.
We had to take our coats off outside of the courtroom doors.
And I remember dropping my coat on chairs just outside the doors, and showing my pass, and the MP-- or the US military, not MP, partic-- not MP for that-- that duty-- opened the door and I went in.
And I saw, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] immediately facing me as I went into the courtroom, were the four judges on the bench, who tried the doctors, who heard the Doctors' Trial.
There were three judges and an alternate.
American judges.
And--
The chief judge was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Walter Beals of Seattle, a distinguished jurist.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let me see.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Sebring was one of the others.
And I have all of this in my mind.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Swearingen-- Victor Swearingen was the alternate.
And the third regular judge I-- I have in my notes here, but I just don't remember at this moment.

I-- of course, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember whether my very first trip into that courtroom was the first court reporter's turn, as we say, of the day, which would be before they actually started the proceedings.
That I do not remember, whether it was the first turn or whether I went in.
It was a rotating system with six court reporters going into the courtroom for, as I remember, it was either-- we started out at half-hour turns at a time.
And we relieved that reporter who was in there at the end of a sentence by an understanding, a tap on the shoulder or whatever, and that reporter got up and left.
And we did this on a revolving basis all day long.
So we-- I would be back into the courtroom, and then toward the end of the day, we-- we went in for 15-minute periods to cut the time, so that that transcript could be finished more quickly.
But I do remember the-- there was a balcony built in the courtroom for the-- the people who were admitted, just the general audience.
But they had to be cleared to get into that courtroom.

When-- from when we finished that one turn, left, we walked past the prisoner's dock, close enough to touch Karl Brandt, the first defendant in the box, or Hermann Goering, in the case of those reporters.

And we had to walk from our dais where we sat to report the testimony, which was just to the left of the witness box, and to the right of the prosecuting attorneys and all of their desks, and the translators and the-- the prosecution team, the lawyers, and to the left of the pris-- of the German defense counsel, who were all seated in about three rows, as I remember, in front of the prisoner's dock.
And out, and the guard-- in that case, the American MPs on the inside of the courtroom, opened the door.
And we went out.

I picked up my coat, walked down a long hall to-- a cold hall-- to our office, where I had a typewriter-- as we each did, each of the reporters-- and immediately sat down and typed that half hour of transcript.
And we were told not to worry about making corrections if we made mistakes typing.
These were manual typewriters.
That all we were to do was X out the mistake and start the word again and, you know, hope to get it right the second time.
And it was a very tight operation, as soon as we had that turn.
And then we had a schedule so we could-- we-- we knew when we were due back in that courtroom again, you know, for the entire day and the days to come.
Working on that transcript.
Oh yes, yes.
Oh, yes.
Not with all of our reporters In that office, the 20-plus, and I have the names of every single one of them.

But in my case, somehow I became very friendly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with a Hawaiian court reporter, Piulani Ahuna of Honolulu, whose folks were educators in Hawaii, and claimed to be one of the-- the only remaining 88 pure-blooded Hawaiian families.
And especially Siegfried Ramler, who was born in Jerusalem, educated in London, spoke eight languages fluently who was an interpreter.
And there were several other reporters, maybe five or six of us, who became very friendly.
And we also bonded with some of the international press, became very friendly with the BBC reporter I told you about.
And there were several others-- So we-- yes, we did.
We had this group.
I think they were not only maybe Allied personnel who were able to get passes to the courtroom, but German citizens.
They wanted-- I think they wanted to have German citizens, who had never been members of the Nazi party, who had been cleared of Nazism, in the courtroom to hear what we all were going to hear.
And the-- the international press.
There was a tremendous international press team there, and they were seated in the regular audience section.
It was this-- this special-- I think that I have that right.
I know there was a balcony built.
And I believe the balcony was for the-- the private-- public, not private, but public people who were admitted to the courtroom, and that the lower level was the international press.
That area was not a really large area.
The courtroom was just taken up with all of the defendants and the counsel, American teams, defense attorneys.

And then we also had seated next to us, a German monitor for the American court reporters who had been-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one could never have been a member of the Nazi party to be on the Allied payroll.
And we had a wonderful monitor who was a German national, seated next to us.
And next to him were the German court reporters.
And we did not have-- the order was, no fraternization.
So we did not mix at all with any of the-- the indigenous population.
Our boss was General Telford Taylor, who was one of the major prosecutors on Goering's trial, under Robert Jackson.
And he was the Chief Prosecutor for the 12 subsequent trials, of which the Nazi Doctors' Trial was the first.
And there were Drexel Sprecher, S-P-R-E-C-H-E-R.
We was from, I think, Washington DC.
Also American, oh, yes.
And he was not military.
General Taylor was the military person.
There were other military officers who were involved in the pre-trial interrogations.
Colonel Amen, I remember, for one.
And we had-- there was a prosecutor-- let me see-- Benjamin Ferencz, F-E-R-E-N-C-Z.
I don't remember that he was assigned specifically to the Doctors' Trial.
These attorneys were not someone that I really knew.
McHaney was another prosecutor.
But there were many, many.
Not in the 12 subsequent trials, other than, at the end of Goering's trial, which was an international trial with the four countries, the judges.
Four judges, one from each of the victorious powers, the United States, France, Great Britain-- representing Great Britain and Northern Ireland-- and the Soviet Russia.
Four judges and four alternate judges, so there were actually eight judges on the bench in Goering's trial.
At the end of that trial, the British, French, and Soviets packed up and went home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do not really know the reason, other than it might have been-- very well have been economic.
The charter had established the International Military Tribunal.
But the London charter, which had been agreed to by the four victorious powers.
So-- but many of the British-- British, yes-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] French language experts stayed on to work on the 12 subsequent trials.
So there were a lot of Allied personnel there.

It was a system that was set up that-- that has been copied-- that was copied in the United Nations, in New York, with-- there was a dial on the front of our desk with-- a large dial, six or eight inches around.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm sorry.
Not in perimeter, but with six channels.
[DRINKS] [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] Excuse me.
I hope I'm going to be all right.
And of course, we had to wear earphones.
And I had my channel turned to the English channel.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The people-- the Germans, of course, had theirs turned to German.
And those were the two major languages that were involved in the 12 subsequent trials.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- if the questions-- if we had a German witness on the stand, which was usually the case, survivors or witnesses [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] speaking in German, we had a box of-- a glassed-in interpreter's box [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] interpreters.
And I know in the major trial, because of the four languages-- three languages-- involved, they had 12 interpreters at all times, interpreting.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember how many interpreters were required for the Doctors' Trial.
But it was a substantial team of interpreters.
And the question was being asked by the American prosecutor of the German witness, then we had to wait.
I mean, we knew English, so we wrote the question immediately as we heard the American prosecutor ask it.
And then we had to wait for the interpreters to interpret the answer, given in German, through our earphones.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And that took a little bit of time.
I did not know German.
But I understood that I-- we had all understood that they had to wait until the end of the sentence to get the-- is it the subject?-- before they could interpret.
So it was interpreting in kind of a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what word to use.
They had to wait.
And then there was a fast interpretation of the answer, and we wrote it down in English.

By the same token, if the witness was an English witness, or supposing the person was a French witness speaking in French, and the-- being cross-examined by a German attorney in German, it was the same situation.
The-- we did not know either one of these languages, although the French usually spoke English.
And so it was a-- a double-waiting for interpretation, from French to English, from German to English.
And by the same token, from German to French, French to German.
I thought it worked quite well.
I understood that, in Goering's trial, there were an awful lot of glitches and getting it-- the reporters-- there was a lot of static.
I mean it was a-- it was a new system that had to be created for those trials, and-- to work all the bugs out.
But I don't remember a great deal of difficulty in our case.
